---
title: "The American Dream, Audited"
format:
  html:
    page-layout: article
    toc: false
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
import json
from pathlib import Path
_p = Path("data/processed/headline_stats.json")
_stats = json.loads(_p.read_text()) if _p.exists() else {}
def S(key, default="-"):
    v = _stats.get(key, default)
    if isinstance(v, (int, float)):
        return f"{v:,.0f}"
    return v
```

```{=html}
<div class="kicker">A Data-Driven Audit · DSAN Scholarship Project · Spring 2026</div>
```

# The American Dream, Audited

::: {.deck}
Three lives, four demographics, six decades of US economic data. What the promise paid out, and to whom.
:::

::: {.byline}
A DSAN scholarship submission · Georgetown University · Spring 2026<br>
[Data: FRED · BLS · US Census · NCES · CMS NHE · CDC NCHS · Federal Reserve DFA · UCSB American Presidency Project]{.byline-data}
:::

::: {.dropcap}
In 1965, a 24 year old guy with a high school diploma could buy a house in Cleveland, support a wife at home, and start a pension. By 1965 standards he was nothing special. The American Dream was basically what his employer, his bank, and his country had agreed to hand him in exchange for showing up to work. He was also, almost certainly, white.

This site audits that promise. The question isn't whether the Dream existed. That's a sentiment question, not a measurement one. The question is what it actually paid out, year by year, to the people the country actually had. Men and women, white and Black and Hispanic, fresh out of school and at the end of working life. It uses six decades of public data and one unit, hours of full time labor, to find out.

What the data shows is that the Dream was never one promise. It was a layered one, and the layers came off unevenly. Some people got more of it over time. Others got less. By 2020, even the white men who had it best in 1970 are working twice as long for the same house their parents bought.
:::

::: {.kicker}
The audit, in one chart
:::

```{=html}
<div class="interactive-embed">
  <iframe src="interactive/time_price_lattice.html"
          style="width:100%; min-height:480px; border:none;"
          loading="lazy"
          title="Heatmap of hours of full-time labor required to afford the median US home, by demographic and decade."></iframe>
</div>
```

::: {.figure-caption}
Hours of full time labor required to afford the median US home, averaged within each decade. Read across a row (left to right) for that demographic's trajectory through time. Read down a column (top to bottom) for the gap between demographics in that decade. Source: BLS CPS median weekly earnings (2000+) joined to Census P-38 historical income tables (1967+) at the 2000 boundary, divided into median home sales price (FRED MSPUS). See [Methods](appendix.qmd) for the stitching procedure.
:::

### A note on the unit

The audit measures everything in hours of work, not dollars. The reason is inflation. A median home cost about \$17,000 in 1965 and roughly \$420,000 in 2023, but the dollar of 1965 and the dollar of 2023 are not the same instrument. The official deflators (CPI, PCE, Boskin-adjusted CPI-U-RS) disagree across 60 years by enough that two analysts could reach opposite conclusions while doing competent work. Hours of labor sidestep the deflator entirely. A home that took 5,900 hours of work in 1970 and one that takes 11,800 hours in 2020 are directly comparable. The unit cancels the dollar. What remains is how much of your life a thing costs.

The full derivation, including the caveats this unit does not handle, is in the [Methods page](appendix.qmd).

## Prologue · The Promise

::: {.kicker}
What was sold
:::

The audit measures what the country actually delivered. Before that, it's worth asking what the country said it was delivering. Sixty five years of State of the Union addresses, 1960 to 2024, is the most consistent on-the-record account we have of how each administration described the economic life of its time. The corpus is 66 speeches and several thousand paragraphs. The chart below clusters those paragraphs by topic and shows how each topic's share of presidential attention has shifted across the decades.

```{=html}
<div class="interactive-embed">
  <iframe src="interactive/sotu_rhetoric_river.html"
          style="width:100%; min-height:560px; border:none;"
          loading="lazy"
          title="Topic prevalence in State of the Union addresses, 1960 to 2024."></iframe>
</div>
```

::: {.figure-caption}
Topic share of paragraphs in State of the Union addresses, 1960 to 2024, where topic clusters were learned with BERTopic over sentence-transformer embeddings of every paragraph in the corpus. Each stream is one of the seven largest topic clusters; hover for the top words BERTopic identified for that cluster. Source: 66 SOTU addresses (stdlib-js/datasets-sotu for 1960 to 2021, Miller Center for 2022 to 2024). See [Methods](appendix.qmd) for the modeling pipeline.
:::

Read this as the promise side of the audit. What the language of presidential rhetoric tracks against, and the acts that follow measure, is what was actually delivered.

## Act I · The Starter

::: {.kicker}
Part I, entering adulthood
:::

```{=html}
<span class="persona-tag starter">The Starter · age 22-27</span>
```

### The launchpad

The Starter is who you were just after school. Early-to-mid twenties, working through a first résumé, signing a first lease, making the first decisions whose interest compounded. In 1970 the median Starter walked into a labor market where a full time job bought a median American home in roughly 5,900 hours of labor. Working a standard 2,000 hours a year, he could pay for the house in under three years. Half a century later, in 2025, that same house took 12,275 hours of labor to buy. The house had not changed. His wage, in time terms, had.

```{=html}
<div class="ledger">
  <div class="ledger-cell"><span class="stat">99</span><span class="stat-label">Hours of work a white man needed for one year of public 4-yr tuition in 1970</span></div>
  <div class="ledger-cell"><span class="stat">332</span><span class="stat-label">Hours he needed for the same tuition in 2022</span></div>
  <div class="ledger-cell"><span class="stat">3.4×</span><span class="stat-label">More hours of work</span></div>
</div>
```

Tuition is the simplest line on the Starter's balance sheet because it is the first one. In 1963 a year of in-state public four year tuition and required fees cost \$243 in the dollars of the day. In 2022 it cost \$9,750, the latest year NCES has published. Wages went up too, but not nearly enough to keep the ratio constant.

```{=html}
<div class="interactive-embed">
  <iframe src="interactive/tuition_time_price.html"
          style="width:100%; min-height:540px; border:none;"
          loading="lazy"
          title="Hours of full-time labor required to afford one year of public four-year tuition and fees, by demographic, 1967 to 2022."></iframe>
</div>
```

::: {.figure-caption}
Hours of full time labor required to afford one year of in-state public four year tuition and required fees. Source: NCES Digest of Education Statistics, Table 330.10 (current dollars), divided by demographic-specific median weekly earnings (Census P-38 stitched to BLS LEU at 2000) ÷ 40.
:::

### The starting line, by demographic

The Starter does not enter the labor market on equal footing. In the 1970s a Black woman working full time in the US earned, on average, about 60 percent of what a white man earned for the same hours. The gap closed, slowly, through the 1980s and 1990s. It never closed completely. The chart below tracks nominal hourly wages, by race and sex, across the working life of three generations of Starters.

```{=html}
<div class="interactive-embed">
  <iframe src="interactive/wage_divergence.html"
          style="width:100%; min-height:560px; border:none;"
          loading="lazy"
          title="Nominal hourly wages by race and sex, 1967 to 2025."></iframe>
</div>
```

::: {.figure-caption}
Nominal median weekly earnings of full time wage and salary workers, expressed as an hourly rate (÷ 40). Source: BLS Current Population Survey LEU series for 2000+ joined to Census Historical Income Tables P-38 for 1967+ (white/Black) and 1974+ (Hispanic). Hispanic earnings were not separately tabulated by the federal government before 1972. The gap on the chart's left edge is the data's own silence on that question.
:::

Read the chart not for the rising lines (every line rises in nominal terms) but for the gaps between them. The vertical distance between any two lines at any given year is what an extra hour of work was worth, depending on who you were.

For the Starter, what these wage levels mean in practice is what they buy. A year of public four year tuition and fees cost a white man 99 hours of work in 1970, about two and a half working weeks. The same year, the same education cost a Black woman more than twice as long: 205 hours. By 2022 the white man's tuition burden had climbed to 332 hours. The Black woman's had reached 467. The gap between starting lines did narrow, in percentage terms. In time terms, in weeks of life surrendered before earning anything, every Starter is paying more, and the ones who started behind are still behind.

```{=html}
<div class="act-break">★</div>
```

## Act II · The Builder

::: {.kicker}
Part II, peak earning, raising family
:::

```{=html}
<span class="persona-tag builder">The Builder · age ~35-45</span>
```

### The home that took two incomes

The Builder is the version of you a decade or two into work. Mid thirties to mid forties, peak earning years, the version that gets a house if a house is going to happen. In 1970 the median Builder was a single-earner household. The wife, statistically, was at home. By 2000 that household had two earners on the books. The household wage roughly doubled. The home time price doubled too.

```{=html}
<div class="interactive-embed">
  <iframe src="interactive/time_price_home.html"
          style="width:100%; min-height:580px; border:none;"
          loading="lazy"
          title="Hours of full-time labor required to afford a median US home, by race and sex, 1979 to present."></iframe>
</div>
```

::: {.figure-caption}
Hours of full time labor (at the demographic's median wage) required to afford the median US home. Source: FRED MSPUS (median home sales price) divided by stitched Census P-38 + BLS LEU hourly wage. See [Methods](appendix.qmd).
:::

The labor force participation rate tells the rest of the story. Men's participation fell from 83.4 percent in 1960 to 67.8 percent in 2025. Women's rose from 37.8 percent to 57.3 percent. The total household labor input did not stay flat. It went up. Where one wage used to buy a house, two now have to. Where one wage used to buy a house, two now barely do.

```{=html}
<div class="interactive-embed">
  <iframe src="interactive/labor_force_participation.html"
          style="width:100%; min-height:480px; border:none;"
          loading="lazy"
          title="Civilian labor force participation rate, men vs. women, 1960 to 2025."></iframe>
</div>
```

::: {.figure-caption}
Civilian labor force participation rate, ages 16+, by sex, monthly. Source: BLS via FRED (LNS11300001 men, LNS11300002 women). The total household labor input nearly doubled, while the wage attached to it did not.
:::

This is the part of the audit where the rising wage line in Act I has to be re-read. The Black woman's hourly wage in 2020 is much higher than the Black woman's hourly wage in 1970, in dollars and in time price. But the Black woman in 2020 is also working in a household where her income is structural, not optional. Her wage went up. The household's reliance on her wage went up faster.

### The bills that didn't exist in 1960

Three line items on the modern Builder's balance sheet were either zero or trivial in 1960:

1. **Childcare.** When the second earner in the household started working, the work that had been unpaid (raising children) became a paid line item. Center-based childcare in the US now costs, on average, around 10 percent of household income for two-parent households with two children, and significantly more in coastal cities. In 1960 this was zero, because no one was paying for it. The cost did not appear. It just changed who was paying.
2. **Employer health premiums.** Before Medicare's 1965 launch, most workers had no premium because most workers had no insurance. By 2020 the average employer-sponsored family premium had climbed past \$21,000 a year. The pay stub shows the worker covering only about a third of that, with the employer "paying" the rest. But employers budget in total-compensation terms: every dollar they spend on health insurance is a dollar that doesn't go into wages. Decades of labor-economics research find that when premiums rise, wages rise more slowly by roughly the same amount.[^premium-incidence] The cut never shows up on a paycheck, but it lands on the worker either way. The full \$21,000 is the premium. The accounting just hides most of it.
3. **401(k) contributions.** In 1960 the median large employer offered a defined-benefit pension funded by the company. The worker's job was to show up. The pension was a deferred wage the company paid. By 2020 that arrangement was gone for most workers. The 401(k) replaced it. The worker now funds their own retirement out of current take-home pay, with the company sometimes matching. The Builder of 1960 did not pay this line. The Builder of 2020 does, because the alternative is no retirement at all.

None of these three line items show up in CPI as a single basket category. All three are real. The Builder of 2020 has a household budget that is structurally different from the Builder of 1960's, even before either of them buys groceries.

### Healthcare: the cost of staying alive

The single sharpest price divergence in the audit's six decade window is between medical care and the price of everything else. Indexed to 1960, the general consumer price level had multiplied by 11 by early 2026. Medical care had multiplied by 27. The gap between the two (the shaded area in the chart) is the slice of the household budget that healthcare has eaten that no other category has.

```{=html}
<div class="interactive-embed">
  <iframe src="interactive/medical_vs_cpi.html"
          style="width:100%; min-height:480px; border:none;"
          loading="lazy"
          title="Medical care prices vs. all consumer prices, both indexed to 1960=100."></iframe>
</div>
```

::: {.figure-caption}
Both indices set to 100 in January 1960. Source: BLS via FRED (CPIAUCSL all items, CPIMEDSL medical care). The shaded area marks the cumulative excess of medical price inflation over general inflation.
:::

The 2025 Builder spends a much larger share of their household budget on insurance, prescriptions, deductibles, and premium contributions than the 1960 Builder did. Some of that buys real medical care that did not exist in 1960 (it would be wrong to ignore this). Some of it is administrative overhead that does not buy any care at all. The audit cannot quality-adjust the medical CPI here, so it does not try. What it can show is the slope. The slope of the medical line is steeper, and stays steeper, than the slope of any other major price category in the basket.

```{=html}
<div class="act-break">★</div>
```

## Act III · The Finisher

::: {.kicker}
Part III, the runway
:::

```{=html}
<span class="persona-tag finisher">The Finisher · age 65+</span>
```

### How long the Dream had to last

The Finisher is who you are after the working years end. Sixty five and beyond. The retirement runway is the time you have between the last paycheck and the last day. For the 1960 Finisher the runway was short. For the 2020 Finisher it has stretched. But the stretching has not been even.

A white man born in 1960 could expect to live 67.4 years. A Black man born the same year could expect 61.1. A six-year gap in the runway, baked in at birth, before either of them had worked a day. By 2017 the white man's life expectancy at birth had risen to 76.4 years. The Black man's had risen to 71.9. Both went up. The gap closed, from 6.3 years to 4.5 years. It did not close completely.

```{=html}
<div class="interactive-embed">
  <iframe src="interactive/longevity_gap.html"
          style="width:100%; min-height:540px; border:none;"
          loading="lazy"
          title="Life expectancy at birth by race and sex, 1960 to 2017."></iframe>
</div>
```

::: {.figure-caption}
Life expectancy at birth, by race and sex, 1960 to 2017. The shaded band marks the white-vs-Black gap among women. Source: CDC NCHS National Vital Statistics System via the CDC open-data portal. Hispanic-specific life expectancy was not separately reported across the full window and is not shown.
:::

The story for women is similar in shape, but the gap closure is the audit's most genuinely good-news finding. White women born in 1960 could expect 74.1 years. Black women, 66.3. An eight-year gap. By 2017 white women's life expectancy had reached 81.2 and Black women's had reached 78.5. The gap had narrowed to under three years. The narrowing is real. It also took 60 years.

For retirement-planning purposes, what matters is not just how long you live but how long the money has to last after you stop earning. A retirement plan built for a 67 year average life expectancy looks very different from one built for an 80 year average. The longer runway is the gift. The cost of stocking the runway is what comes next.

### Savings, social security, and the home as 401(k)

Most American retirements are funded by three things. Social Security, retirement accounts (pension or 401(k)), and home equity. The first two have well-known balance sheets. The third is the quiet one. For most middle-class households, the home is the largest single financial asset they own. Home equity is the de facto retirement account.

This means: if you never bought a home, your retirement is structurally different. And homeownership has not been distributed evenly across the demographic cells of this audit. The Federal Reserve's wealth share data tells the story most starkly.

```{=html}
<div class="interactive-embed">
  <iframe src="interactive/wealth_share.html"
          style="width:100%; min-height:540px; border:none;"
          loading="lazy"
          title="Share of US household net worth held by White, Black, Hispanic, and Other racial groups, 1989 to 2025."></iframe>
</div>
```

::: {.figure-caption}
Share of total US household net worth, by race, quarterly from 1989 Q3 to 2025 Q4. Source: Federal Reserve Distributional Financial Accounts. The "Other" category combines Asian, Pacific Islander, American Indian, multiracial, and other-race households as published by the Fed.
:::

In 1989 (the first quarter the Federal Reserve tracks wealth by race), white households held 90.3 percent of US household net worth. Black households held 4.0 percent. By the end of 2025, white households held 83.5 percent. Black households held 3.3 percent. Black share went down. The 1989 racial wealth gap was already large. Thirty six years later, on this measure, it is wider.

The Asian American share grew most of the white loss (the Federal Reserve's "Other" category, which is dominated by Asian American households, went from 3.6 to 10.8 percent). Hispanic share stayed roughly flat. Most of the wealth growth that did flow out of the white share over the last three decades did not flow to the demographic cells with the most ground to make up.

For retirement specifically, the implication is direct. If wealth is the safety net under Social Security, and the safety net is held disproportionately by one racial group, then a retirement plan that worked for the average American in 1965 is not a retirement plan that works for the average American in 2025. It is a retirement plan that works very well for some Americans and not at all for others.

## The Counterfactual

::: {.kicker}
Part IV, the gap that wasn't closed
:::

The audit's findings to this point are descriptive. They say what happened. The counterfactual is where the audit becomes normative. Here is the same data set against three different stories about what could have happened.

```{=html}
<div class="interactive-embed">
  <iframe src="interactive/counterfactual.html"
          style="width:100%; min-height:600px; border:none;"
          loading="lazy"
          title="Black household share of US net worth: actual vs three counterfactuals."></iframe>
</div>
```

::: {.figure-caption}
The actual line (red) is the Federal Reserve DFA series for Black households' share of US household net worth, 1989Q3 to 2025Q4. The three counterfactual lines are constructed from the same DFA dataset. See [Methods](appendix.qmd) for the formulas.
:::

Each line is a different version of "fair." The 13 percent line is the population-parity benchmark. Black Americans are about 13 percent of the US population, so a wealth share that matched population share would sit at 13 percent. The gold dashed line is what the Black household share would look like if it had grown at the same rate as the "Other" category (mostly Asian American households) grew between 1989 and 2025. The pale-blue dash-dotted line is the most conservative counterfactual: just hold the 1989 racial wealth gap exactly where it was. No closure, just no widening.

The actual line, in red, sits below all three counterfactuals. It does not approach population parity. It does not match the Asian American growth rate. It does not even hold the 1989 gap. It widens it. The choice of which counterfactual to use as a benchmark is normative. The position of the actual line below all three of them is not.

## What the Audit Found

::: {.kicker}
Findings
:::

1. **The white man of 2020 has it twice as hard as the white man of 1970.** In 1970, the median white man working full time could buy the median US home with 5,903 hours of labor, just under three working years. By 2020 he needed 11,823 hours, close to six. The doubling is not an inflation artifact (the time-price unit excludes inflation by construction). It is driven by home prices rising twice as fast as median wages, even for the demographic at the centre of the original Dream.

2. **The wage gap narrowed in percentage terms, less so in lived time.** A Black woman in 1970 worked 12,235 hours for the median home. A white man worked 5,903. The ratio was 2.1. By 2020 it had narrowed to 1.45, a 30 percent closure. But the absolute gap fell only about 16 percent: 6,332 hours in 1970, 5,313 in 2020. The reason the two numbers diverge: both demographics now work more hours for the same house than they did in 1970 (a 2020 Black woman needs 17,136 hours, a 2020 white man 11,823), but the white man's burden grew faster than the Black woman's. The gap shrank partly because his line caught up to hers, not because hers eased.

3. **Medical prices grew 2.4 times faster than the rest of the basket.** Indexed to 1960, general consumer prices had multiplied by 11 by early 2026. Medical prices had multiplied by 27. No other major CPI category has this slope. The medical premium is now a structural price tax that the 1960 Builder did not pay.

4. **The wealth gap has not narrowed since 1989.** White households held 90.3 percent of US household net worth in 1989 Q3. By 2025 Q4 they held 83.5. Black households went from 4.0 percent to 3.3. Most of the white loss flowed to Asian American households (the Fed's "Other" category, which grew from 3.6 to 10.8 percent). On the wealth side, the post-Civil Rights catch-up the data tracks since 1989 has not happened for Black households on a measurable basis.

5. **Black women's life expectancy gain is the audit's most positive finding.** The 1960 Black woman could expect 66.3 years. The 1960 white woman, 74.1. An 8-year gap. By 2017, the gap had closed to 2.7 years. The narrowing is real, structural, and the only place in the audit where the gap has clearly closed. It took six decades.

## Recommendations

::: {.kicker}
Where the evidence points
:::

### For policymakers

The wage-side measures of the racial gap have moved more than the wealth-side measures over the past sixty years. Federal policy that targets wage gaps (anti-discrimination law, equal-pay enforcement) has produced measurable narrowing. Federal policy targeting wealth gaps has not, on the data here. Policymakers concerned with the racial wealth gap should examine whether the lever that closed the wage gap, direct enforcement, has an equivalent in the wealth space: enforcement of fair-housing and fair-lending law, transfer-tax structures, and downpayment subsidy programs that target the demographic cells where wealth share has actually fallen since 1989. Our analysis found that the wealth gap has been more durable to general policy than the wage gap.

### For employers

The implicit subsidy that companies once gave younger workers (a starting wage, on-the-job training, time to grow into the role) has shifted into a transfer arrangement where the worker now pays for their own onboarding through the time price of their first apartment, first commute, and first health premium. Companies competing for early-career talent should examine whether their compensation structure has implicitly assumed a 1980 cost of living. Our analysis found that the time price of a Starter's first year of working life has risen faster than the Starter's wage in every demographic cell.

### For individuals

The audit cannot give a 1960-equivalent life back to anyone, and it does not pretend to. What it can offer is a framing. The comparison most people make ("am I doing better than my parents did?") is the wrong unit, because it conflates inflation with affordability with quality changes with everything else. The right unit is time price: how many hours of your work does a thing cost? An individual who knows the time price of their housing, healthcare, childcare, and education, in their own labor units, has a clearer picture of what their next decade of work is actually buying than someone who only knows the dollar values.

## A note on what this audit is not

::: {.kicker}
Honest framing
:::

This is an audit of measurable, monetizable economic conditions. The American Dream has always meant more than that. It has meant clean air, longer leisure, safer neighborhoods, freedom of movement, and less back-breaking work. Many of those things have improved over six decades. They do not show up in the price tables.

The audit is not a verdict on whether modern American life is "better" than 1965 American life. It is a measurement of what the economic terms of the contract have done. Some of those terms have improved. Most of the ones this audit can measure have gotten harder for most people, and they have gotten harder unevenly.

If there is one takeaway it is the unevenness. The numbers in this audit are what they are. The interpretation is that the United States entered the past sixty years with one promise and finished them with several different promises, depending on who you were when you started. The data does not say whether that is a failure of policy or a feature of the country. It says only that it is, and that it should be measured.

[Continue to [Methods](appendix.qmd).]{.byline}

[^premium-incidence]: The theoretical case is Lawrence Summers, "Some Simple Economics of Mandated Benefits," *American Economic Review* 79(2), 1989. The most-cited empirical estimate, which finds that rising health premiums are offset roughly dollar-for-dollar by slower wage growth, is Katherine Baicker and Amitabh Chandra, "The Labor Market Effects of Rising Health Insurance Premiums," *Journal of Labor Economics* 24(3), 2006. Jonathan Gruber and Alan Krueger (1991) reach similar conclusions for workers' compensation, and Gruber (1994) for mandated maternity benefits.
